items.name: SignalingHub
items.description: WebRTC signaling hub. Central WS broker that owns the room registry, attaches peers, validates JSEP messages, and routes offer / answer / ICE traffic. Transport-agnostic — bind to `app.ws()` in production, an `EventEmitter` shim in tests. SDP validation is cross-browser by design: session-level `a=fingerprint` / `a=ice-ufrag` / `a=ice-pwd` lines are accepted as a fallback for media sections that omit their own copy (RFC 8839 §5.4, RFC 8122 §5). This is required for Firefox interop — Firefox emits `a=fingerprint` only at session level.

examples.title: Bind a hub to an `app.ws()` route with all production knobs
Observe lifecycle events

options.notes: Hard cap on offer / answer size (bytes).
Hard cap on `a=candidate:` lines per SDP.
Per-peer signaling message rate, msg / sec.
Disconnect after this many malformed frames.
Max attaches per IP per minute.  `0` disables.

If set, transports whose `info.origin` is not
on this list are rejected at attach time.

If set, every `join` must include a valid
JWT signed with this secret and audience `room:<name>`.
If false, joins targeting an unknown room are rejected.

Default room topology. `auto` starts every room
as full mesh and promotes to `sfu` once the peer count
exceeds `maxMeshPeers`. Pure signaling is unchanged;
the hint is broadcast to peers and surfaced via
`hub.stats()` so clients can switch transports.

Peer count at which an `auto` room is promoted from
mesh to SFU (and at which a `mesh` room emits
`peer:limit:reached` for capacity alarms).

Optional SFU adapter or spec string (memory|mediasoup|livekit|<pkg>).
Mounts a `hub.media` facade backed by this adapter so
application code can drive routers/transports/producers
through the hub without importing the adapter directly.
Adapter constructor options, forwarded when `opts.sfu` is a string.

methods.description: Live peer count across all rooms (and unattached).
Get or lazily create a room.

Attach a transport (WS connection or mock) as a new signaling peer. Wires up message and close handlers, performs origin / IP-rate pre-checks, sends a `hello` frame with the new peer id, and returns the `Peer`.  The returned peer is also registered in `hub.size`. Called from your `app.ws()` upgrade handler in production: ```js app.ws('/rtc', (ws, req) => hub.attach(ws, { user:   req.user, ip:     req.ip, origin: req.headers.origin, })); ```

examples.title: Test harness: attach a mock transport

methods.description: Shut down every peer and clear every room.
Snapshot of hub state for dashboards and health checks. Includes the configured topology defaults, current peer/room counts, and a coarse media-plane summary when an SFU adapter is mounted.

methodReturns.type: Promise<{topology:string, maxMeshPeers:number, peers:number, rooms:object[], mediaPlane:object | null}>

items.name: Room
items.description: Room / channel abstraction for the signaling hub. Holds a set of peers plus a fluent chain of policy gates (`require()`, `canPublish()`, `canSubscribe()`). Constructed lazily via `hub.room(name)`; pair with `useCluster()` for multi-node membership.

options.notes: Owning hub.

params.notes: Room name, used as routing key.

methods.description: Mark the room as public.  Returns `this` for chaining.
Set the publish-permission check.  Hub calls this before relaying offers.
Set the subscribe-permission check.  Hub calls this before relaying answers.
Current member count.
Evaluate every `require()` gate against the candidate peer.

Force the room's topology and broadcast the change to every peer. Pure signaling: the hub does not redirect media; client SDKs are responsible for switching transports when the new topology is received via `room-topology`.
Send a `{type, ...payload}` JSON frame to every peer in the room.

methodParams.notes: Optional peer id to skip (e.g. the originator).

methods.description: Kick every peer with code 1001 (going-away) and unregister from the hub.

items.name: Peer
items.description: Per-connection state machine for a signaling peer. Wraps a `{ send, on, close }` transport (typically `app.ws()`'s `WebSocketConnection`) and exposes JSEP message types as events. Constructed by `hub.attach(transport, info)` — not directly.

examples.title: Inspect every newly-attached peer

options.notes: Authenticated user object (if any).
Remote IP for audit / rate limits.

methods.description: Send a JSON envelope to this peer.  `type` is added to `payload` and the result is serialised once.  Silently drops sends after close.
Send a typed error frame.  Callers SHOULD use this rather than throwing, because exceptions escape the message handler and tear down the process.
Close the underlying transport with a WebSocket-style code and reason. Defaults to 1000 (normal closure).

items.name: SDP Parser
items.description: Zero-dependency RFC 8866 SDP parser and serializer with WebRTC-specific attribute extraction per RFC 8829 (ice-ufrag, ice-pwd, fingerprint, setup, mid, rtcp-mux, rtpmap, fmtp, ssrc, etc.). Pure structure — policy lives in the signaling layer.

options.notes: Reject payloads larger than this.

methods.description: Serialize a `SessionDescription` back to RFC 8866 text with CRLF line endings.  The serializer is round-trip safe for documents produced by `parseSdp`: it emits the raw attribute list verbatim so any media-level attribute we did not lift into a structured field is still preserved.

examples.code: const sdp = stringifySdp(parseSdp(offer.sdp));

items.name: ICE Candidates
items.description: Zero-dependency ICE candidate parser, serializer, and address classifiers (private / loopback / link-local / mDNS) per RFC 8839, plus a `filterCandidates` helper used by `SignalingHub` to enforce privacy-preserving policies on relayed offers/answers.

params.notes:
Candidate line, e.g.
`candidate:842163049 1 udp 1677729535 192.168.1.5 50000 typ host`.

items.example:
const c = parseCandidate('candidate:1 1 udp 2122194687 1.2.3.4 50001 typ srflx raddr 192.168.1.5 rport 50000');
  if (c.type === 'relay') { console.log('relay candidate'); }

examples.code:
const c = parseCandidate('candidate:1 1 udp 2122194687 1.2.3.4 50001 typ srflx raddr 192.168.1.5 rport 50000');
  if (c.type === 'relay') { console.log('relay candidate'); }

methods.description: Serialize a parsed candidate back to its canonical line format. Round-trips outputs of `parseCandidate` exactly, including the insertion order of `extensions`.

examples.code: const out = stringifyCandidate(parseCandidate(line));

methods.description: True for RFC 1918, RFC 6598 (CGNAT), and IPv6 ULA (RFC 4193) addresses.
True for IPv4 127.0.0.0/8 and IPv6 ::1.
True for IPv4 169.254/16 and IPv6 fe80::/10.
True for mDNS `.local` hostnames used by browsers to avoid leaking local IPs (Chrome's mDNS ICE candidates - RFC 8624 / draft-ietf-mmusic-mdns-ice-candidates).

Filter an array of candidates (lines or parsed objects) against a policy. Returns the same shape it was given: if you pass strings you get strings back; if you pass parsed objects you get parsed objects back.  Unparseable string lines are silently skipped so a single bad candidate never poisons the whole offer.

methodReturns.description: Surviving candidates, same element shape as input.

items.name: STUN Client
items.description: Zero-dependency RFC 8489 STUN client. Sends a Binding Request over UDP, parses the Binding Response, and returns the server-reflexive address from XOR-MAPPED-ADDRESS (or legacy MAPPED-ADDRESS). NAT-discovery subset only; TURN allocations live in `lib/webrtc/turn/`.

params.notes: 12-byte ID; one is generated if omitted.

methods.description: Discover the public (server-reflexive) address by sending a STUN Binding Request to `opts.host:opts.port`. Retries with the same transaction ID until a matching Binding Success is received or `retries` attempts elapse.  Closes the socket on resolve/reject.
methods.example: const { address, port } = await stunBinding({ host: 'stun.l.google.com', port: 19302 });

examples.code: const { address, port } = await stunBinding({ host: 'stun.l.google.com', port: 19302 });

methods.description: Parse a STUN message (header + attributes).
Encode an XOR-MAPPED-ADDRESS attribute body (no TLV header).
Decode an XOR-MAPPED-ADDRESS attribute body to `{family, address, port}`.

items.name: TURN Credentials
items.description: RFC 7635 ephemeral TURN credentials. Generates short-TTL username/credential pairs accepted by any compliant TURN server (notably `coturn` with `use-auth-secret`). Returns an `RTCIceServer`- shaped object ready to hand to browsers.

examples.title: Hand a fresh credential to a browser before it joins a room
Multiple URLs (UDP, TCP, TLS) for failover
Use directly in an RTCPeerConnection (browser side)

items.name: TURN Server
items.description: Zero-dependency embedded TURN server (RFC 5766). Pairs with `issueTurnCredentials` for long-term-credential auth and implements UDP allocations, permissions, Send/Data indications, channel bindings, lifetimes, and per-user quotas. TCP/TLS listeners are reserved and currently throw `TURN_TRANSPORT_UNSUPPORTED`.

examples.title: Boot an embedded TURN server alongside Zero Server
Issue creds + return them with the room join payload
Multi-listener (UDP + future TCP) with tight per-user quotas

options.notes:
Shared HMAC secret for ephemeral credentials produced by
{@link module:webrtc/turn/credentials.issueTurnCredentials}.

Address bound by each per-allocation relay socket.  Production
deployments should pass the server's public IP.

methods.description: Bind all configured listeners.  Resolves once every listener is listening; rejects on the first bind error.
Close all listeners and free every allocation's relay socket.
The bound address of the first listener (handy for tests that ask the kernel for an ephemeral port via `port: 0`).

items.name: SFU Adapter
items.description: Pluggable Selective Forwarding Unit (SFU) adapter interface. The signaling hub stays media-agnostic; the adapter owns routers, transports, producers, and consumers. Ships with `MemorySfuAdapter`, `MediasoupSfuAdapter`, and `LiveKitSfuAdapter`. Subclass `SfuAdapter` for custom backends.

examples.title: Select an adapter at boot via env
Wire an SFU into the signaling hub per room

methods.description: Switch a consumer to the given simulcast spatial / temporal layer.
Set consumer priority (1-255, higher = more bandwidth budget under congestion).
Ask the SFU to forward a PLI/FIR to the producer the consumer is bound to.
Pause an individual consumer (stop forwarding to a single subscriber).
Resume a previously paused consumer.
Apply incoming/outgoing bitrate hints to a transport.
Create an SCTP data producer on a transport.
Create an SCTP data consumer bound to `dataProducerId`.
Start an audio-level observer on a router. Emits `audio-level` adapter events. Returns `{ id, close() }`.
Start an active-speaker observer. Emits `active-speaker` adapter events. Returns `{ id, close() }`.
Pipe a producer from this adapter's local router into another router (possibly on a remote SFU node). Returns `{ pipeProducerId, pipeConsumerId, localRouterId, remoteRouterId }`.

methodGroups.group: Targeted stats (Phase 2 surface; coarse stats() remains for back-compat)

methods.description: Return native stats for a single producer.
Return native stats for a single consumer.
Return native stats for a single transport.
Toggle low-level trace event emission on a router (mediasoup `trace` events: 'probation', 'bwe', 'rtp', 'keyframe', etc.).
Register a handler invoked as `(event, payload)` for adapter-level events ('producer-new', 'producer-pause', 'consumer-new', 'transport-close', 'router-close', etc.). Returns an unsubscribe function.
Lazy-load and instantiate an SFU adapter.

one of:
- an object exposing the SfuAdapter contract (returned as-is),
- 'memory' | 'mediasoup' | 'livekit' | adapter package id.
constructor options forwarded to the adapter.

Pre-constructed instance (e.g. shared singleton in tests)

  const sfu = loadSfuAdapter('@acme/zero-server-sfu-janus', { wsUrl: 'ws://janus/ws' });
examples.title: Third-party adapter package

methods.description: Override to create a routing context for a single room.
Override to allocate a WebRTC transport for a peer in a router.

items.name: Memory SFU Adapter
items.description: In-process "memory" SFU adapter. Bookkeeping-only passthrough that records producers/consumers and emits adapter events without forwarding media packets. Ideal for unit tests, CI, and local dev — use a native adapter for real media plane work.

examples.title: Use the memory adapter inside a vitest suite
Drive it through `loadSfuAdapter`

items.name: Mediasoup SFU Adapter
items.description: mediasoup-backed SFU adapter (peerDependency on `mediasoup`). Wraps a `Worker` plus one `Router` per `createRouter()` call and delegates produce/consume/pause/resume/close/stats to the native objects. Adapter events are uniform via `onEvent`.

examples.title: Production setup with custom RTP port range and announced IP
One router per room, lazy on first join
Inject a stub for unit tests

options.notes: Injected mediasoup module (testing); defaults to `require('mediasoup')`.
Pre-created `mediasoup.Worker`; bypasses the lazy worker bootstrap.
Forwarded to `mediasoup.createWorker(...)`.
Default router media codecs.
Default `router.createWebRtcTransport(...)` options.
Pre-created `WebRtcServer` to share a single UDP/TCP port across workers (k8s / single-IP).
`{ listenInfos: [...] }` forwarded to `worker.createWebRtcServer(...)` on first use.

methods.description: Best-effort shutdown: closes every router, then the worker if we own it.

items.name: LiveKit SFU Adapter
items.description: LiveKit-backed SFU adapter (peerDependency on `livekit-server-sdk`). Maps the `SfuAdapter` contract onto LiveKit's remote media plane: `createTransport` mints an AccessToken, mute/close delegate to `RoomServiceClient`, and produce/consume are local bookkeeping while LiveKit handles media client-side.

examples.title: Cloud LiveKit project
Self-hosted LiveKit + handing the JWT to a browser
Mute a noisy publisher (REST passthrough)

options.notes: LiveKit server URL (wss://...).
LiveKit API key.
LiveKit API secret.
Injected `livekit-server-sdk` module (testing).
Pre-built `RoomServiceClient` (testing).
Forwarded to `createRoom()` when fields are missing.
Default `{canPublish, canSubscribe, ...}` for minted tokens.
AccessToken TTL.

methods.description: Fetch one room from LiveKit by name.  Returns the matching entry from `client.listRooms([name])` or `null`.
Create a LiveKit room.  `opts.name` overrides the auto-generated name. Returns a router handle whose `id` is the room name.
Mint an AccessToken for `peer` to join the LiveKit room.  Returns a transport handle containing the JWT and URL the peer hands to the LiveKit client SDK.

items.name: Bot Peer (wrtc)
items.description: Server-side WebRTC peer ("bot") built on the `wrtc` peerDependency. `spawnBotPeer({ hub, room, ... })` attaches an in-process peer that joins a room and drives a real `RTCPeerConnection` per remote peer. Bidirectional — use for recording, transcription, AI participants, or SFU verification.

methods.description: Spawn a server-side bot peer that joins `room` on the given hub.

methodOptions.notes: Opaque user object attached to the peer.
IP recorded on the attached peer.
Optional join token forwarded to the hub.

examples.title: Recording bot: dump every inbound audio track to a file
AI participant: push synthesized audio back to the room
Inject a fake `wrtc` for unit tests

items.name: Join Tokens
items.description: Signed, short-TTL JWT join tokens scoped to `room:<name>`. HS256 by default, RS256 supported. Verification is constant-time and surfaces every failure mode as `WebRTCError({ code: 'INVALID_TOKEN' })`. The hub auto-enforces tokens when constructed with `joinTokenSecret`.

options.notes: HMAC secret (HS256) or PEM key (RS256).
User identifier (string) or object containing `id`.
Target room name.

Seconds until expiry.  Negative values are accepted
(used by tests to mint already-expired tokens).
Override the default `room:<name>` audience.
Additional claims merged into the payload.

examples.title: Browser receives a token from a regular HTTP route

methods.description: Verify a join token and return its payload.  Throws a `WebRTCError` with `code: 'INVALID_TOKEN'` on any failure - bad signature, expired, audience mismatch, malformed, etc.

examples.title: Manually verify a token (most apps never need this — the hub does it)
Allow a 30-second clock skew between issuer and verifier

items.name: Observability
items.description: Optional metrics + tracing wiring for a `SignalingHub`. `bindObservability(hub, { metrics, tracer })` exports the standard `zs_webrtc_*` Prometheus series plus OTel-compatible spans. Both adapters are duck-typed and opt-in.

options.notes: Prometheus-compatible registry.
Tracer for span emission.

examples.title: Wire metrics + tracing from Zero Server's observe scope
Metrics only (no tracer)
Custom prom-client registry adapter

items.name: E2EE Key Relay
items.description: End-to-end-encrypted key relay for WebRTC. Publishers wrap SFrame/Insertable-Streams keys in sealed envelopes (X25519 + HKDF-SHA-256 + AES-256-GCM) and broadcast via `e2ee-key`; the hub stays opaque. `E2eeChannel` accepts any opaque `Buffer`, so libsignal or NaCl-sealed payloads work out of the box.

methods.description: Generate a fresh X25519 keypair suitable for {@link sealKey} / {@link openSealedKey}.
methods.example:
const { publicKey, privateKey } = generateE2eeKeyPair();
  const wireKey = publicKey.export({ format: 'jwk' }).x; // base64url

examples.code:
const { publicKey, privateKey } = generateE2eeKeyPair();
  const wireKey = publicKey.export({ format: 'jwk' }).x; // base64url

methods.description: Seal an opaque byte string for a single recipient using the project's default envelope (X25519 ECDH + HKDF-SHA-256 + AES-256-GCM). Envelope layout: `[ver:1] [ephPubRaw:32] [nonce:12] [ciphertext:N] [tag:16]`

methodParams.notes:
Recipient's X25519 public
key (KeyObject or 32-byte raw).

methods.example:
const sealed = sealKey(Buffer.from(sframeKey), bob.publicKey);
  peer.e2ee.publish(epoch, sealed);

examples.code:
const sealed = sealKey(Buffer.from(sframeKey), bob.publicKey);
  peer.e2ee.publish(epoch, sealed);

methods.description: Open an envelope produced by {@link sealKey} with the recipient's private key.  Throws if the envelope is malformed, was sealed for a different recipient, or was tampered with in flight.

examples.code: const sframeKey = openSealedKey(received.key, bob.privateKey);

methods.description: Broadcast a sealed key to the rest of the peer's room.

methodReturns.description: The epoch the key was published under.

methods.example:
const sealed = sealKey(sframeKey, bob.publicKey);
  const epoch  = peer.e2ee.publish(null, sealed);

examples.code:
const sealed = sealKey(sframeKey, bob.publicKey);
  const epoch  = peer.e2ee.publish(null, sealed);

methods.description: Receive sealed keys published by *other* peers in the same room.

methodParams.type: (ev: {from: string, epoch: number, key: Buffer}) => void

methods.description: Install an {@link E2eeChannel} on a peer as `peer.e2ee`.  Idempotent: a second call returns the existing channel.

examples.code: hub.on('join', ({ peer }) => attachE2ee(peer, hub));
examples.title: Attach E2EE on every join

items.name: Cluster Adapter
items.description: Cluster adapter for the signaling hub. `useCluster(hub, adapter)` glues a `SignalingHub` to any `{ publish, subscribe }` pub/sub bus so joins, leaves, broadcasts, and direct frames flow across nodes. Ships with `MemoryClusterAdapter`; wire to Redis, NATS, Kafka, or any in-house bus in production.

options.notes:
Stable id for this node.  Defaults to
a random 8-byte hex string.

Region tag (e.g. `'us-east'`).  Surfaces
in load announcements and lets the
cascade's bridge selector prefer
same-region peers.

`() => { cpu?:number, producers?:number,
consumers?:number, bandwidthIn?:number,
bandwidthOut?:number, custom?:object }`.
Sync or async.  When provided, the
coordinator publishes a load snapshot
every `opts.loadIntervalMs` ms.

0 disables the periodic timer
(callers can call `publishLoad()` manually).

methods.description: Look up the node that owns a remote peer, if any.
Forward a direct frame to a peer on another node.  Called by the hub when a routed message (`offer` / `answer` / `ice`) targets a peer id that is not in the local registry.

methodReturns.description: `true` if a remote node was addressed.

methods.description: Mirror a `room.broadcast(...)` to peers in the same room on other nodes.  Called automatically from `Room#broadcast`.

Publish a load snapshot now.  Returns the snapshot that was sent. Called automatically on the configured interval when `loadProbe` is set, but can also be invoked manually (e.g. from tests, or to publish immediately after a producer count changes).
Snapshot of every known node (including self).

methodReturns.type: Array<{nodeId:string, region:string | null, load:object | null, lastSeen:number}>

methods.description: Pick a node for a new bridge using one of the built-in selectors or a custom comparator.  Returns the chosen `nodeId` (which may be this node's own id when it wins).
Tear down all subscriptions and clear remote state.
Attach a cluster adapter to a `SignalingHub`.

examples.title: In-memory cluster (tests / single-process simulation)
Redis adapter (BYO ioredis)

items.name: Cascade Coordinator
items.description: Cross-node SFU cascade orchestrator. Sits on top of a {@link useCluster}-backed {@link SignalingHub} and a local {@link SfuAdapter} so a single room can span multiple hub nodes' media planes. Each node owns a local Router (a "bridge") for its subset of peers; producers are piped to every peer bridge so consumers on any node see every publisher. See [`docs/scopes/webrtc-scaling.md`](../../docs/scopes/webrtc-scaling.md) for the wire protocol, capacity model, and migration matrix.

examples.title: Two nodes, one virtual room
Register a bridge as rooms get created

options.notes: Falls back to the cluster nodeId or a random id.
SfuAdapter; defaults to `hub.sfu` (the one the hub was built with).
PipeTransport listen info advertised to peer bridges.

methods.description: Register the local bridge for `room`.  Idempotent: subsequent calls return the cached entry.  `router` is the handle returned by `sfu.createRouter()`; producers created on that router will be fanned out to every peer bridge.
Tear down the local bridge for `room`.  Closes every pipe opened to peer bridges and announces `bridge:close` so peer nodes drop their mirrored state.

Announce that a local producer has been created on the bridge for `room` so peer bridges open a `pipeToRouter` consuming it.  Called automatically when the SFU emits `producer-new` and the producer's transport belongs to a known bridge router.
Tear down fanout for a local producer.
Resolve a producer id to its remote origin, if any.  Returns null when the producer is local or unknown.
Snapshot of the cascade state for observability.
Tear down every bridge and stop processing bus messages.

Attach a {@link CascadeCoordinator} to a hub that already has a cluster adapter bound via {@link useCluster}.  Stores it at `hub._cascade` and returns the coordinator so callers can `registerLocalBridge(room, router)` as rooms get created.

items.name: MCU Adapter
items.description: Optional MCU (multipoint control unit) layer that sits on top of an {@link SfuAdapter}. Where an SFU forwards each publisher's stream untouched, an MCU mixes them down to a smaller number of composite tracks (one audio mix + one tiled video) so receivers only have to decode O(1) streams instead of O(N). The right topology for SIP gateways, low-end clients, regulated recording, and ultra-large rooms where consumer decode cost is the bottleneck. zero-server ships no native mixer — JS-side Opus/H264 transcoding is CPU-prohibitive. The MCU layer is a contract: adapters can spawn ffmpeg (the included {@link FfmpegMcuAdapter}), shell out to a GPU-backed mixer, or call into a managed service. The {@link MemoryMcuAdapter} is bookkeeping only for tests and capacity planning.

examples.title: Bookkeeping-only mixer (tests, capacity planning)
Real audio mixing via ffmpeg

options.notes: Bound SfuAdapter, used by subclasses that need to ingest/produce.
Adapter name, defaults to the constructor name.

methods.description: Snapshot of active mixes.
Tear down every active mix.

items.name: Ffmpeg MCU Adapter
items.description: Spawns one ffmpeg child process per mix and implements the {@link McuAdapter} contract. The integrator wires mediasoup `PlainTransport` RTP feeds into ffmpeg via `opts.inputs` and consumes ffmpeg's output via `opts.outputs`; the adapter only owns the child lifecycle. Gated on `ffmpeg-static` (or an explicit `{ ffmpegPath }`) so missing binaries fail loudly. For a fully managed equivalent prefer LiveKit egress via {@link LiveKitSfuAdapter}.

examples.title: Spawn an audio mix

options.notes: Overrides the `ffmpeg-static` lookup.
Injected for tests (defaults to `child_process.spawn`).

methods.description: Spawn an ffmpeg process to mix the given producers.  The caller is responsible for wiring the SFU's PlainTransport RTP feeds into ffmpeg via `opts.inputs` (an array of `{ producerId, sdp, host, port }`) and consuming ffmpeg's output via `opts.outputs`.

methodParams.notes: `{ producerIds, inputs, outputs, kind, layout, args? }`

items.name: Recording Manager
items.description: Adapter-agnostic recording / egress / ingress facade. {@link RecordingManager} auto-detects which pipeline the bound {@link SfuAdapter} supports (`livekit` egress, `ffmpeg` child, or `memory` bookkeeping) and exposes a uniform `startRecording` / `stopRecording` surface. {@link IngressManager} wraps the adapter's `createIngress` / `deleteIngress` calls for WHIP / RTMP / SIP / URL-pull sources.

examples.title: Auto-pipeline recording on top of any adapter
Force the ffmpeg pipeline
Create a WHIP ingress and route it into a room

options.notes: SfuAdapter (or anything exposing egress methods).
For `pipeline: 'ffmpeg'` (defaults to `child_process.spawn`).

methods.description: Start a new recording for `roomName`.  The `pipeline` field selects the backend: - `'livekit'` — call `adapter.startRoomCompositeEgress`. - `'livekit-track'` — call `adapter.startTrackEgress`. - `'ffmpeg'` — spawn an ffmpeg child process (requires `{ ffmpegPath }` or `ffmpeg-static`; caller wires RTP). - `'memory'` — bookkeeping only (default when no other backend can be inferred).
Stop an active recording by id.  Idempotent.

methodReturns.description: true if the recording was stopped here.

methods.description: List every recording the manager knows about.
Create an ingress (WHIP / RTMP / URL pull / SIP).
Tear down an ingress by id.  Idempotent.
List every known ingress.
Aggregate counts for observability.
Stop every active recording.  Safe to call during shutdown.
Tear down every active ingress.  Safe to call during shutdown.

items.name: WebRTC CLI
items.description: CLI subcommands for the `zs webrtc:*` namespace. Pure-function entry point `runWebRTCCommand(subcmd, flags, deps)` so the dispatch can be exercised in tests without spawning a child process or hitting the network.  All side effects (stdout / stderr / process.exitCode) are injected through `deps`, defaulting to the real globals when called from `lib/cli.js`.

returns.description: The exit code that would have been set.
